questions.question: Match each attention component to its role.
categories: Asks 'what am I looking for?'
Says 'what do I contain?'
Provides the actual information to be retrieved
The probability score indicating how much to focus on each token

correct_mapping.Value (V): Provides the actual information to be retrieved
correct_mapping.Attention weight: The probability score indicating how much to focus on each token

questions.explanation: In the attention mechanism, the Query represents what a token is searching for, the Key represents what each token offers for matching, and the Value carries the actual information. The attention weight is the softmax-normalized score from the Query-Key dot product, indicating how much each Value should contribute to the output.

questions.question: Match each concept to how it handles word meaning.
categories: Fixed vector per word, same regardless of context
Different vector for the same word depending on surrounding context
Sparse binary vector with a 1 in one position, no semantic information
Counts word frequencies, ignores word order entirely

correct_mapping.Word2Vec: Fixed vector per word, same regardless of context
correct_mapping.Transformer embeddings: Different vector for the same word depending on surrounding context
correct_mapping.One-hot encoding: Sparse binary vector with a 1 in one position, no semantic information
correct_mapping.Bag of words: Counts word frequencies, ignores word order entirely

questions.explanation: Word2Vec learns dense fixed embeddings -- 'bank' always gets the same vector. Transformers produce contextualized embeddings where 'bank' gets different vectors in 'river bank' vs 'bank account.' One-hot encoding uses a sparse vector with no semantic meaning. Bag of words counts word occurrences without considering order.

questions.question: Match each architecture to how it processes sequential data.
categories: All tokens processed in parallel via self-attention
Tokens processed sequentially with gating mechanisms for long-range dependencies
Tokens processed sequentially but struggles with long-range dependencies
Processes local windows of tokens with shared filters

correct_mapping.Transformer: All tokens processed in parallel via self-attention
correct_mapping.LSTM: Tokens processed sequentially with gating mechanisms for long-range dependencies
correct_mapping.Simple RNN: Tokens processed sequentially but struggles with long-range dependencies
correct_mapping.Convolutional layer: Processes local windows of tokens with shared filters

questions.explanation: Transformers process all tokens simultaneously through self-attention, enabling parallelism. LSTMs process sequentially but use gates (forget, input, output) to better handle long-range dependencies. Simple RNNs process sequentially but suffer from vanishing gradients over long sequences. Convolutional layers look at local windows of tokens.

questions.question: Match each step of scaled dot-product attention to what it does.
categories: Compute raw relevance scores between all token pairs
Prevent scores from becoming too large for stable learning
Convert raw scores into probabilities that sum to 1
Produce the final output as a weighted combination of information

correct_mapping.Q * K^T: Compute raw relevance scores between all token pairs
correct_mapping.Divide by sqrt(d_k): Prevent scores from becoming too large for stable learning
correct_mapping.Softmax: Convert raw scores into probabilities that sum to 1
correct_mapping.Multiply by V: Produce the final output as a weighted combination of information

questions.explanation: The attention formula works in four steps: first compute dot products between Queries and Keys to get raw scores, then scale by sqrt(d_k) to keep values manageable, then apply softmax to get proper probability weights, and finally multiply by Values to produce the output.

questions.question: Match each Transformer block to its components.
categories: Self-attention + feed-forward network
Masked self-attention + cross-attention + feed-forward network
Queries from decoder, Keys and Values from encoder
A small neural network applied independently to each token

correct_mapping.Cross-attention: Queries from decoder, Keys and Values from encoder
correct_mapping.Feed-forward sublayer: A small neural network applied independently to each token

questions.explanation: The encoder has two sublayers: self-attention and a feed-forward network. The decoder has three: masked self-attention, cross-attention (connecting to the encoder), and a feed-forward network. Cross-attention uses decoder states as Queries and encoder outputs as Keys and Values. The feed-forward network processes each token position independently.

questions.question: Match each property to whether it belongs to Transformers or LSTMs.
items: Hidden state passed from step to step
categories: Transformer
LSTM
Transformer
LSTM
questions.explanation: Transformers process all tokens in parallel using self-attention, where each token directly attends to every other token. LSTMs process tokens one at a time sequentially, passing a hidden state from one time step to the next. Both architectures are designed for sequential data and can capture long-range dependencies, but they do so in fundamentally different ways.

questions.question: Match each attention type to its description.
categories: Each token attends to all tokens in the same sequence
Decoder tokens attend to encoder output tokens
Tokens can only attend to earlier positions, not future ones
Multiple parallel attention operations capturing different relationship types

correct_mapping.Self-attention: Each token attends to all tokens in the same sequence
correct_mapping.Cross-attention: Decoder tokens attend to encoder output tokens
correct_mapping.Masked self-attention: Tokens can only attend to earlier positions, not future ones
correct_mapping.Multi-head attention: Multiple parallel attention operations capturing different relationship types

questions.explanation: Self-attention operates within a single sequence. Cross-attention connects two sequences, typically letting the decoder attend to encoder outputs. Masked self-attention blocks future positions for autoregressive generation. Multi-head attention runs several attention operations in parallel with different learned projections to capture diverse relationships.

questions.question: Match each problem to the solution the Transformer provides.
items: Same word has different meanings in different contexts
Need to focus on different relationship types simultaneously
categories: Self-attention processes all tokens in parallel
Direct attention between any two tokens regardless of distance
Contextualized embeddings change based on surrounding words
Multi-head attention with different learned projections

correct_mapping.Sequential processing is slow: Self-attention processes all tokens in parallel
correct_mapping.Hard to capture long-range dependencies: Direct attention between any two tokens regardless of distance
correct_mapping.Same word has different meanings in diff: Contextualized embeddings change based on surrounding words
correct_mapping.Need to focus on different relationship: Multi-head attention with different learned projections

questions.explanation: Transformers solve several key problems: parallel processing replaces slow sequential computation; self-attention creates direct connections between any two tokens (no matter how far apart); contextualized embeddings capture different word meanings; and multi-head attention captures multiple relationship types at once.

questions.question: Match each concept to what happens without it.
categories: Dot products become very large, softmax produces extreme values
Raw scores are not proper probabilities and don't sum to 1
Only one type of relationship is captured per layer
Decoder can cheat by looking at future tokens during training

correct_mapping.Scaling by sqrt(d_k): Dot products become very large, softmax produces extreme values
correct_mapping.Softmax in attention: Raw scores are not proper probabilities and don't sum to 1
correct_mapping.Multi-head attention: Only one type of relationship is captured per layer
correct_mapping.Masked self-attention in decoder: Decoder can cheat by looking at future tokens during training

questions.explanation: Each component addresses a specific need: scaling prevents numerical instability from large dot products; softmax converts raw scores into proper probability weights; multi-head attention enables capturing diverse relationship types; and masking ensures the decoder cannot peek at future tokens, which would break autoregressive generation.

questions.question: Match each attention context to where the Queries, Keys, and Values come from.
categories: Q, K, V all from encoder input
Q, K, V all from decoder input (future positions blocked)
Q from decoder, K and V from encoder output
Query from decoder hidden state, K and V from encoder hidden states

correct_mapping.Encoder self-attention: Q, K, V all from encoder input
correct_mapping.Decoder masked self-attention: Q, K, V all from decoder input (future positions blocked)
correct_mapping.Decoder cross-attention: Q from decoder, K and V from encoder output
correct_mapping.RNN attention (Bahdanau): Query from decoder hidden state, K and V from encoder hidden states

questions.explanation: In encoder self-attention, all three vectors come from the same encoder input. In decoder masked self-attention, all three come from decoder input but future positions are masked. In cross-attention, Queries come from the decoder while Keys and Values come from the encoder. In classic RNN attention, the decoder hidden state acts as the query against encoder hidden states.
